_meta.description: Plain-English explainers for common Maryland municipal-budget terms. Powers the 'what does this mean?' tooltips in the Sykesville Budget Explorer. Written to be read by a resident with no finance background.
_meta.audience: Sykesville residents, no finance background assumed
_meta.source_note: Definitions reflect Maryland municipal finance conventions (fiscal year runs July 1 – June 30; tax rates expressed per $100 of assessed value).

general_fund.plain_english: The town's main checking account. Almost all day-to-day money — taxes in, salaries and services out — flows through here.
general_fund.why_it_matters: When people say 'the town budget,' they usually mean the General Fund. It's where your property tax dollars land and get spent.
general_fund.analogy: Like your household checking account, as opposed to a special savings jar set aside for one purpose.

enterprise_fund.plain_english: A separate account for a service that pays for itself through user fees rather than taxes — most commonly water and sewer.
enterprise_fund.why_it_matters: Your water bill funds this, not your property tax. It's kept separate so the town can see whether the utility covers its own costs.
enterprise_fund.analogy: Like running a small business inside the government: the water system is supposed to break even on the fees it charges.

fund_balance.plain_english: Money the town has saved up and not yet spent — its reserves or rainy-day savings.
fund_balance.why_it_matters: A healthy fund balance means the town can absorb emergencies (a snowstorm, a broken water main) without scrambling. Rating agencies and residents both watch it.
fund_balance.analogy: Your emergency savings account. A common guideline is keeping several months of expenses on hand.

unassigned_fund_balance.plain_english: The portion of savings not already promised to a specific purpose — the most flexible reserve money.
unassigned_fund_balance.why_it_matters: This is what the town can actually tap in a pinch. It's the truest measure of financial cushion.
unassigned_fund_balance.analogy: The part of your savings that isn't already earmarked for the car payment or the vacation.

operating_budget.plain_english: The plan for ongoing, year-to-year costs: salaries, fuel, electricity, supplies, routine maintenance.
operating_budget.why_it_matters: This is the recurring cost of keeping the lights on and the services running. It repeats every year.
operating_budget.analogy: Your monthly bills — rent, groceries, utilities — as opposed to a one-time big purchase.

capital_budget.plain_english: Money for big, long-lasting investments — a new fire truck, repaving a road, a park renovation. The 'CIP' is the Capital Improvement Program, the multi-year plan for these projects.
capital_budget.why_it_matters: Capital spending is lumpy and often borrowed for, so it's tracked separately from everyday costs.
capital_budget.analogy: Buying a car or replacing your roof — a one-time investment that lasts for years, not a monthly bill.

debt_service.plain_english: The yearly payments — principal plus interest — the town makes on money it borrowed, usually for big capital projects.
debt_service.why_it_matters: Like a mortgage payment, it's a fixed obligation that comes before discretionary spending. High debt service leaves less room for everything else.
debt_service.analogy: Your monthly mortgage or car-loan payment.

property_tax.plain_english: A tax based on the assessed value of land and buildings you own. In Maryland it's expressed as a rate per $100 of assessed value.
property_tax.why_it_matters: It's typically the single largest source of money a town controls directly, and the one most visible to homeowners.
property_tax.analogy: If your home is assessed at $300,000 and the town rate is $0.33 per $100, you'd owe about $990 in town property tax (300,000 ÷ 100 × 0.33).

income_tax.plain_english: A share of the state income tax that Maryland returns to local governments based on where residents live.
income_tax.why_it_matters: It rises and falls with residents' earnings, so it's a big but less predictable revenue source than property tax.
income_tax.analogy: A slice of the income tax you already pay the state gets routed back to fund your local services.

highway_user_revenue.plain_english: State money — funded mainly by the gas tax and vehicle fees — sent back to towns specifically to maintain local roads.
highway_user_revenue.why_it_matters: It's earmarked for streets and transportation. When the state cuts it, towns feel it in their road-repair budgets.
highway_user_revenue.analogy: A dedicated allowance from the state that can only be spent on roads.

recordation_tax.plain_english: A tax charged when real-estate documents — deeds and mortgages — are officially recorded, usually when a property is bought or refinanced.
recordation_tax.why_it_matters: It tracks the housing market: strong when homes are selling, weak when the market slows. That makes it volatile.
recordation_tax.analogy: A fee collected at the closing table when a house changes hands.

admissions_amusement_tax.plain_english: A small tax on the price of certain entertainment — event tickets, recreation, some amusements.
admissions_amusement_tax.why_it_matters: A minor but steady revenue source tied to local entertainment activity.
admissions_amusement_tax.analogy: A few cents on the dollar added to a ticket or recreation fee.

intergovernmental_revenue.plain_english: Money the town receives from other governments — state or federal grants and shared taxes.
intergovernmental_revenue.why_it_matters: It lets a small town fund projects it couldn't afford alone, but it often comes with strings attached and can disappear when the grant ends.
intergovernmental_revenue.analogy: A matching gift or scholarship: helpful, but you can't count on it forever.

assessed_value.plain_english: The dollar value the state assigns to your property for tax purposes. Maryland reassesses every property on a three-year cycle.
assessed_value.why_it_matters: Your tax bill = assessed value × the tax rate. When assessments rise, bills can rise even if the rate stays flat.
assessed_value.analogy: The 'sticker price' the state puts on your property; the tax rate is the percentage applied to it.

constant_yield_tax_rate.plain_english: The tax rate that would raise the same total dollars as last year, even though assessments changed. Maryland requires towns to publicize when they exceed it.
constant_yield_tax_rate.why_it_matters: If the town keeps the old rate while assessments rise, it collects more money — effectively a tax increase. The constant yield rate is the honest baseline for comparison.
constant_yield_tax_rate.analogy: The 'no real increase' rate. Charging above it means residents pay more in total, even if the headline rate didn't move.

fiscal_year.plain_english: The town's 12-month budgeting year. In Maryland it runs July 1 through June 30. 'FY2026' means July 1, 2025 – June 30, 2026.
fiscal_year.why_it_matters: Budgets, audits, and comparisons are all organized around this window, not the calendar year.
fiscal_year.analogy: Like a school year that starts in fall and ends in spring — a yearly cycle that doesn't line up with January–December.

general_obligation_bond.plain_english: A loan the town takes out by selling bonds to investors, backed by its full taxing power, usually to pay for major capital projects.
general_obligation_bond.why_it_matters: It spreads the cost of a big, long-lived asset over the years that residents will use it — but it commits the town to years of debt-service payments.
general_obligation_bond.analogy: Taking out a mortgage to buy a house instead of paying the whole price up front.

balanced_budget.plain_english: A budget where planned spending does not exceed expected revenue plus any savings the town chooses to use.
balanced_budget.why_it_matters: Maryland local governments are generally required to adopt balanced budgets — they can't plan to spend money they don't have.
balanced_budget.analogy: Not planning to spend more than you bring in, unless you're deliberately dipping into savings.
